tweets.text:
Aggressive selling continues in tech stocks:

The technology sector ETF, $XLK, posted -$8.7 billion in outflows over the last month, the largest withdrawal of any S&P 500 sector.

This was followed by the energy sector ETF, $XLE, at -$1.0 billion, and the communication services sector ETF, $XLC, at -$500 million.

The financial sector ETF, $XLF, attracted +$2.1 billion in inflows, the largest intake among all sectors.

Furthermore, the healthcare ETF, $XLV, and the utilities ETF, $XLU, attracted +$800 million and +$600 million in inflows, respectively.

Healthcare, energy, and financial ETFs have been the best-performing sectors over the last month, gaining +7.4%, +6.3%, and +4.5%, respectively.

Over the same period, $XLK has declined -5.4%, making it the worst-performing sector.

Technology stocks are taking a breather.

tweets.text:
Shocking stat of the day:

The 3-week volatility of momentum stocks relative to the S&P 500 volatility is up to a record 4.0x.

This group includes high-growth technology stocks at the center of the AI boom like Nvidia, $NVDA, Super Micro Computer, $SMCI, Palantir, $PLTR, D-Wave Quantum, $QBTS, and CoreWeave, $CRWV.

This figure has more than QUADRUPLED over the last several weeks.

By comparison, this ratio peaked at ~2.0x during the 2020 pandemic crash and ~1.8x during the Dot-Com bust.

This comes as the index of US momentum stocks is down -24% so far in July, on track for its biggest monthly decline since the 2008 Financial Crisis.

The market's biggest winners are rapidly falling out of favor.

tweets.text:
US corporate executives are increasingly selling their own stock:

Stock sales of corporate insiders rose +20% YoY in the first half of 2026, to $77.6 billion, the largest half-year total since H1 2021.

This is also the 2nd-largest half-year total in over 20 years.

By comparison, insider purchases totaled just $6.9 billion over the same period, slightly above the 7-year low of $6.7 billion set in 2025.

In other words, corporate executives sold more than 11 shares for every 1 share they bought during the first 6 months of the year.

This comes as insiders are increasingly concerned over geopolitical risks, elevated valuations, and the sustainability of AI spending.

Corporate insiders are locking in historic gains.

tweets.text: On this day in 1968, Robert Noyce and Gordon Moore left Fairchild Semiconductor to found Intel, backed by $2.5 million that venture capitalist Arthur Rock raised in a single afternoon of phone calls, largely on the strength of the two founders' reputations. Noyce had co-invented the integrated circuit, and Moore had already articulated the observation that would become Moore's Law: that the number of transistors on a chip would roughly double every two years. Intel's first products were memory chips, but a 1971 contract for a Japanese calculator company led it to build the 4004, the world's first commercial microprocessor, a device that put an entire computer's processing power on a single sliver of silicon. That decision reorganized the whole electronics industry around the microchip. The global semiconductor industry Intel helped create now generates over $600 billion a year.
tweets.full_text: On this day in 1968, Robert Noyce and Gordon Moore left Fairchild Semiconductor to found Intel, backed by $2.5 million that venture capitalist Arthur Rock raised in a single afternoon of phone calls, largely on the strength of the two founders' reputations. Noyce had co-invented the integrated circuit, and Moore had already articulated the observation that would become Moore's Law: that the number of transistors on a chip would roughly double every two years. Intel's first products were memory chips, but a 1971 contract for a Japanese calculator company led it to build the 4004, the world's first commercial microprocessor, a device that put an entire computer's processing power on a single sliver of silicon. That decision reorganized the whole electronics industry around the microchip. The global semiconductor industry Intel helped create now generates over $600 billion a year.

thread_tweets.text:
@DBonatOliv @RayThisLife02 Standardness rules are set at the node level. Users can set them to what they want, including to 2023 defaults.

Regarding spam, you’re the one that brought up space reduction. But there is no material space reduction for spam in this fork where it’s cheapest (the witness).

It’s a relay policy that each node has control over for themselves.

thread_tweets.text:

Monetary use is currently low, which means low fees and low costs of spam.

thread_tweets.text:
@AndiBasha3 @Bongo_King1 All else being equal, I’d like to see less non-monetary data in bitcoin. If someone proposes a way to materially reduce it with few trade-offs, I’d be happy to see it.

But no, I’m not concerned by it.

tweets.text: @Vladcostea Often the case in crypto is that those who launch coins, VCs who fund those tokens and get out quick, and power-traders all make money, with a big extraction from retail that gets left with the bags in permanent decline.

tweets.text: Good Morning from Germany, where the state is investing, but companies still aren’t. Public investment has surged to 125% of its 2019 level, while private investment has fallen to 88%. One year after “Made for Germany,” corporate spending is rising, but far too slowly to hit the €631bn target by 2028. Germany is becoming more expensive without becoming more attractive. (HT CEO Table)
tweets.full_text: Good Morning from Germany, where the state is investing, but companies still aren’t.
